title: Phrasal Verbs in Daily Situations
learningObjectives: Learn phrasal verbs used in common daily situations
Understand phrasal verbs in short stories and dialogues
Practice recognizing separable and inseparable phrasal verbs
Build vocabulary for talking about routines and activities

sections.title: Phrasal Verbs Around Us
sections.content:
Phrasal verbs are everywhere in English! When you talk to friends, watch movies, or read messages, you hear phrasal verbs all the time. At the A2 level, you can learn more phrasal verbs to talk about your daily life, work, and hobbies.

Let's explore some common ones in real situations.

sections.title: At Work
sections.content:
Sarah works in an office. Every morning, she <logs in> to her computer and checks her emails. She often has to <deal with> problems from customers. When she finishes a task, she <moves on> to the next one. Sometimes her boss asks her to <fill in> a form or <look into> a complaint.

Work phrasal verbs:
• <Log in> - enter a computer system
• <Deal with> - handle a problem
• <Move on> - go to the next thing
• <Fill in> - complete a form
• <Look into> - investigate something

sections.title: Going Shopping
sections.content:
At the store, you <try on> clothes before you buy them. If something is too expensive, you <put it back>. When you find something you like, you <pick it out>. At the checkout, you <pay for> your items and the cashier <wraps them up>.

Shopping phrasal verbs:
• <Try on> - test clothes by wearing them
• <Put back> - return something to its place
• <Pick out> - choose something
• <Pay for> - give money for something
• <Wrap up> - cover in paper

sections.title: Conversation Examples
sections.content:
Example 1: <I need to drop off this package at the post office.> (drop off = deliver)

Example 2: <Can you look after my cat this weekend?> (look after = take care of)

Example 3: <We ran out of milk. Can you buy some?> (run out of = have no more)

sections.title: At Home
sections.content:
Home life is full of phrasal verbs:

Cleaning: <Tidy up> your room. <Throw away> old things. <Put away> your clothes.

Cooking: <Heat up> the soup. <Cut up> the vegetables. <Wash up> the dishes after dinner.

Relaxing: <Lie down> on the sofa. <Put on> some music. <Switch off> your phone.

Phrasal verbs at home:
• <Tidy up> - Make a place neat and organized
• <Throw away> - Put in the rubbish bin
• <Heat up> - Make food warm
• <Cut up> - Cut into small pieces
• <Wash up> - Clean the dishes
• <Lie down> - Rest in a horizontal position

sections.title: Social Situations
sections.content:
When you meet people, you use many phrasal verbs:

• <Get along with> - Have a good relationship with someone
• <Look forward to> - Feel excited about something in the future
• <Catch up with> - Talk to someone you haven't seen recently
• <Drop by> - Visit someone without planning
• <Show up> - Arrive at a place
• <Dress up> - Wear nice or formal clothes

Example: <I get along with my neighbours very well. They sometimes drop by for coffee. I'm looking forward to their barbecue next weekend.>

sections.title: Phrasal Verbs in Stories
sections.content:
Example 1: WEEKEND - <On Saturday, I tidied up my flat and threw away some old magazines. Then I dressed up and went out to meet my friends.>

Example 2: COOKING - <First, cut up the onions. Then heat up some oil in a pan. When the food is ready, wash up the dishes.>

Example 3: VISITING - <My friend dropped by yesterday. We caught up over coffee and talked about our holidays. I'm looking forward to seeing her again.>

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Remember:
• Phrasal verbs are used in every situation: work, shopping, home
• Some phrasal verbs can be separated: <pick it out> or <pick out a shirt>
• Three-word phrasal verbs like <look forward to> cannot be separated
• Practice by using phrasal verbs when you describe your day
• Read and listen to English to find new phrasal verbs naturally

tooltips.definition: Choose or select something from a group
Choose a shirt from several options
To feel excited about something in the future

keyVocabulary.definition: To handle or manage a problem
keyVocabulary.example: I have to deal with many emails every day.

keyVocabulary.definition: To complete a form or document
keyVocabulary.example: Please fill in this application form.
The manager will look into the problem.

keyVocabulary.definition: To test clothes by wearing them
keyVocabulary.example: Can I try on this dress?

keyVocabulary.definition: To deliver or leave something somewhere
keyVocabulary.example: I'll drop off the books at the library.

keyVocabulary.definition: To take care of someone or something
keyVocabulary.example: She looks after her younger brother.

keyVocabulary.definition: To have no more of something
To feel excited about something in the future
keyVocabulary.example: I'm looking forward to the weekend.

grammarPoints.explanation: Some phrasal verbs can be separated by an object: <pick out a shirt> or <pick a shirt out>. Others cannot be separated: <look after the baby> (NOT <look the baby after>).
examples: Try on the jacket / Try the jacket on (separable)
Fill in the form / Fill the form in (separable)
commonMistakes: Separating inseparable verbs: 'look the baby after' — Wrong! Say 'look after the baby'
